H1_space_gap.description: RepSim (repr-space) vs TRAK (param-space) on standard scoring
H1_space_gap.summary: PASS (2/3): RepSim dominates on counterfact (+32.4pp) and ftrace (+16.6pp), but TRAK dominates on toxicity (-24.0pp). H1 is SUPPORTED with a task-dependent caveat on toxicity.

H2_contrastive_asymmetry.description: Contrastive gain larger in parameter-space than representation-space
H2_contrastive_asymmetry.pass_criterion: TRAK gain > RepSim gain on >= 2/3 tasks

toxicity.notes: Both gains are 0 (mean-subtraction preserves rank ordering at pilot scale)

counterfact.notes: Both gains are 0 (mean-subtraction preserves rank ordering at pilot scale)

ftrace.notes: Both gains are 0 (mean-subtraction preserves rank ordering at pilot scale)

H2_contrastive_asymmetry.summary: INCONCLUSIVE: Contrastive scoring produced zero metric change for both methods. Mean-subtraction shifts scores but preserves rank ordering exactly, so rank-based metrics (AUPRC, Recall@K) are invariant. This is a fundamental property of rank-based evaluation, NOT evidence against H2. Full-scale experiments with per-query bootstrap and continuous metrics are needed.

H3_orthogonality.description: FM1 and FM2 corrections approximately additive (low interaction)

toxicity.notes: Interaction is exactly 0 because contrastive scoring produced identical metrics. Orthogonality is trivially satisfied but not meaningfully tested.

counterfact.notes: Interaction is exactly 0 because contrastive scoring produced identical metrics. Orthogonality is trivially satisfied but not meaningfully tested.

ftrace.notes: Interaction is exactly 0 because contrastive scoring produced identical metrics. Orthogonality is trivially satisfied but not meaningfully tested.

H3_orthogonality.summary: TRIVIALLY SATISFIED: Interaction is 0.0 on all tasks because contrastive scoring did not change any rank-based metric. The orthogonality test has no statistical power at this pilot scale. Full-scale with continuous metrics needed.

H8_repsim_vs_bm25.pass_criterion: gap >= 10pp on data_selection and toxicity tasks
H8_repsim_vs_bm25.summary: 1/3 tasks pass >= 10pp. RepSim > BM25 on toxicity (+17.6pp) and ftrace (+9.5pp). BM25 achieves perfect R@50=1.0 on counterfact (lexically solvable at pilot scale).

observations: ANOMALY: TRAK (0.9256) > RepSim (0.6852) on toxicity by 24.04pp. This REVERSES the expected H1 direction. Possible explanations: (a) small pilot sample (n=100, only 20 unsafe), (b) toxicity detection may benefit from parameter-space information, (c) AUPRC is highly sensitive to score calibration with few positives.
BM25 achieves Recall@50=1.000 on counterfact. Factual attribution may be solvable by lexical matching at pilot scale (n=100). This triggers the BM25 decision gate for factual_attribution.

Contrastive scoring (mean-subtraction) produced exactly 0.0pp gain across ALL methods and tasks. This is because rank-based metrics (AUPRC, Recall@K) are invariant to the additive shift s_C = s - E[s]. The scores change but relative ordering is preserved. This does NOT falsify H2 -- it reveals a metric limitation at pilot scale.
RepSim strongly dominates on counterfact (R@50=0.994 vs TRAK=0.670, +32.4pp) and ftrace (R@50=0.756 vs TRAK=0.590, +16.6pp). H1 is strongly supported on these two tasks.
kNN (0.809) outperforms RepSim (0.685) on toxicity. Both are representation-space methods; kNN uses nonlinear similarity which may capture toxicity patterns better.

decision_gates.status: NOT TRIGGERED: H3 interaction is 0 (trivially). No evidence for or against orthogonality.
TRIGGERED: BM25 >= RepSim on factual attribution. Restrict CRA claims.
WARNING: BM25 (R@50=1.000) >= RepSim (R@50=0.994) on counterfact. This specific task may be lexically solvable.

recommendations: H1 SUPPORTED on 2/3 tasks. Investigate toxicity reversal (TRAK > RepSim) at full scale.
H2 INCONCLUSIVE: Need continuous metric (e.g., Kendall tau on scores) or larger sample to break rank ties.
H3 TRIVIALLY SATISFIED: Need H2 to produce nonzero gains before interaction can be tested.
H8 PARTIALLY SUPPORTED: RepSim > BM25 on toxicity and ftrace, but BM25 dominates counterfact.
CRITICAL: The toxicity reversal (TRAK >> RepSim) is the most important finding. If it persists at full scale, H1 needs qualification by task type.
RECOMMENDATION: For full-scale, add Kendall-tau or Spearman correlation as continuous metrics to properly test H2 contrastive scoring effect.
